system_context.business_goals: Optimize plant maintenance, ensure safety, and improve efficiency and quality of simulation work.

architectures.name: Client Processing / Distributed Storage
architectures.description: A decentralized architecture where the Client application performs heavy data processing and filtering. Simulation results remain on the specific Unix compute nodes where they were generated, requiring the Client to aggregate data from multiple sources.
architectures.architecture_characteristics: High local compute utilization, high data locality for writes, high network load during client-side aggregation.

key_components.responsibilities: Input data editing, simulation orchestration requests, and heavy post-calculation processing/filtering.
Entry point for simulation requests; manages calculation services on Unix nodes.
Coordinates simulation instances and spawns task processes.

architectures.data_flow: Client sends parameters to SB -> SB triggers CS on a node -> CS spawns TCS -> TCS writes results to local node storage -> Client fetches and processes data from various nodes.
architectural_constraints: Simulations must run on Unix servers.
Exactly one Service Broker per server computer.
Client must have network access and permissions for all compute nodes.
architectures.notes: CAN: Scale compute capacity horizontally by adding nodes. CANNOT: Easily transition to a thin-client/web model without moving processing logic. Accommodating centralized backup is difficult due to distributed data.

scenario_elicitation.elicitation_method: AI-Driven Brainstorming based on System Context

scenarios.scenario_text: A nuclear engineer edits input parameters in the Client Application, submits a simulation to a Unix-based Calculation Server via the Service Broker, and retrieves aggregated results for safety analysis within the established performance threshold.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Directly supported by the current decentralized architecture, though the Client Application bears the heavy processing burden for aggregation.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Ensures the core mission of managing simulations and performing safety-critical analysis is met by the current system.

scenarios.scenario_text: To improve simulation quality, a developer modifies the simulation logic to utilize a new calculation library; this change must be deployed across all decentralized Unix Calculation Servers without requiring a restart of the Service Broker.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect; current architecture requires manual or scripted updates across distributed Unix nodes, which may impact simulation consistency during the rollout.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Directly addresses the need to improve the efficiency and quality of simulation work through continuous software improvement.

scenarios.scenario_text: To optimize plant maintenance schedules, a maintainer updates the system configuration to support a 20% increase in concurrent simulation data storage in the database while maintaining data integrity.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Direct; however, since results remain on compute nodes, the 'storage' is distributed. Scalability depends on individual node disk capacity rather than a central repository.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Focuses on the long-term system goal of optimizing plant maintenance through robust data management.

scenarios.scenario_text: A system administrator migrates the data filtering and heavy processing logic from the Client Application to a centralized service to reduce client-side hardware requirements and network congestion.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect and disruptive; this represents a significant architectural shift from the current 'heavy client' decentralized model to a more service-oriented or centralized model.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Explores ways to improve system efficiency and reduce the maintenance burden of high-spec client workstations.

scenario_classification.rationale: The scenario describes the core operational path of the current architecture where the client handles orchestration and aggregation while Unix nodes handle execution.

While the architecture supports distributed execution, the requirement to deploy new libraries across all nodes without a Service Broker restart implies a need for dynamic loading or a decoupled deployment mechanism not explicitly defined.
Horizontal scalability is a noted strength of this architecture. Increasing storage capacity is achieved by adding more Unix nodes or expanding local storage on existing nodes.
This scenario directly contradicts the 'Client Processing' fundamental design. Moving logic to a centralized service requires a complete redesign of the data flow and component responsibilities.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: None required; the current architecture is built specifically for this workflow.
issues_identified: Performance may degrade if the number of nodes to aggregate from grows significantly, increasing network load on the client.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Implement a plugin-based architecture or versioned execution paths within CS-01. This allows Task processes (TCS-01) to reference new libraries without the parent Service Broker needing a restart to re-bind services.
issues_identified: Potential for version mismatch across nodes during the rollout phase.
Consistency of results if different nodes use different library versions simultaneously.
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Use a shared network file system (NFS) for libraries so all nodes pull the same version simultaneously, though this introduces a single point of failure.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Provision additional Unix compute nodes or upgrade disk hardware on existing nodes to accommodate the 20% increase.
issues_identified: The Client Application must be updated with the network addresses/permissions for any new nodes added to the cluster.
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Implement a cleanup/archival policy for old simulation data to free up 20% capacity on existing nodes without adding hardware.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Refactor C-01 to remove post-calculation logic. Develop a new service tier that aggregates data from nodes and performs filtering. Update SB-01 to route data to this new service instead of directly to the client.
issues_identified: Centralized service becomes a massive performance bottleneck.
Data movement increases significantly as data must move from Nodes to Service then to Client.
Violates the current data locality benefits.
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Implement 'Edge Processing' where a small filtering agent runs on each Unix node to reduce the volume of data sent to the Client, rather than centralizing all logic.

scenario_interaction_weights.rationale: SB-01 is the most critical hotspot as it sits at the intersection of high-risk architectural changes. Scenario S-04 requires a widespread refactor of routing logic that threatens data locality, while S-02 introduces complex service-binding requirements. Failure to address this component correctly risks systemic performance bottlenecks and routing failures across the entire architecture.

CS-01 is hit by three scenarios, primarily impacting scalability and extensibility. While S-01 and S-03 are low effort, S-02 introduces medium-risk versioning issues and potential consistency conflicts. It is critical for the Maintainer and SysAdmin to ensure that plugin-based execution and node provisioning do not lead to fragmented simulation results.

Although hit by fewer scenarios, C-01 is subject to high-risk refactoring in S-04, which involves stripping out post-calculation logic. This directly impacts the End-user experience and requires significant Developer effort. However, it ranks lower than the service tier components because the risk is more localized to the client application rather than the distributed communication backbone.

architectural_strengths: Resilience to core simulation workflows: The architecture is purpose-built for its primary use case (S-01), requiring zero changes to affected components C-01, SB-01, and CS-01 for standard operations.
Efficient resource scalability: The system handles horizontal scaling of compute nodes (S-03) with low effort and risk, supporting business goals for simulation efficiency and capacity.

Data Locality: The current design avoids unnecessary data movement by keeping processing logic close to nodes, a benefit highlighted by the high-risk 'widespread' impact of attempting to centralize that logic (S-04).
architectural_weaknesses: Rigidity in logic distribution: The architecture struggles significantly with redistributing post-calculation logic (S-04), where refactoring leads to high risk, performance bottlenecks, and violations of data locality.

Service Broker Coupling: SB-01 is a critical hotspot (Rank 1) that sits at the intersection of routing and service-binding; it is highly sensitive to changes in library versioning (S-02) and routing refactors (S-04).

Extensibility friction: The requirement for a plugin-based architecture in CS-01 (S-02) indicates that the current service-binding mechanism is not natively flexible enough to handle library updates without potential consistency issues or restarts.
overall_results.modifiability_assessment: The architecture is highly modifiable regarding physical resource scaling but exhibits structural rigidity when attempting to re-tier logic or update shared libraries. It is a 'stable-but-coupled' system that requires careful isolation of the Service Broker and Client components to evolve without systemic failure.

recommendations.recommendation: Adopt 'Edge Processing' agents on Unix nodes instead of a centralized processing service.
recommendations.rationale: Directly addresses the high-risk S-04 scenario by reducing data movement and avoiding the performance bottlenecks associated with a new central tier, thereby protecting simulation quality.

recommendations.recommendation: Refactor SB-01 to decouple routing logic from service-binding execution.
recommendations.rationale: SB-01 is the primary hotspot. Decoupling these concerns reduces the risk of systemic failures during library updates (S-02) or workflow changes (S-04).

recommendations.recommendation: Implement a versioned plugin architecture within CS-01.
recommendations.rationale: Provides a structured way to handle S-02 library updates without service restarts, mitigating the risk of version mismatches across the cluster.

recommendations.recommendation: Automate node registration and implement a data archival policy.
recommendations.rationale: A 'quick win' for S-03 that improves efficiency and reduces the manual effort required when provisioning new compute nodes.
